title: Publish to charmhub
content:
Publish a charm to charmhub #  Publishing to charming is straightforward: all you need to do is follow the register-upload-release workflow.
Register a new charm’s name under your acount #  charmcraft names charmcraft register karma-k8s Pack and upload charm #  charmcraft pack charmcraft upload karma-k8s_ubuntu-20.04-amd64.charm charmcraft revisions karam-k8s Upload resource (e.g. OCI image) #  charmcraft resources karma-k8s charmcraft status karma-k8s docker pull ghcr.io/prymitive/karma:v0.92 IMAGE_ID=$(docker images -q ghcr.io/prymitive/karma:v0.92) charmcraft upload-resource karma-k8s karma-image --image=$IMAGE_ID charmcraft resource-revisions karma-k8s karma-image Release #  charmcraft release karma-k8s --channel=edge --revision=1 --resource=karma-image:1 charmcraft status karma-k8s Publish a bundle to charmhub #  charmcraft names charmcraft register lma-light charmcraft revisions lma-light charmcraft pack charmcraft upload lma-light.zip charmcraft revisions lma-light charmcraft release lma-light --channel=edge --revision=1 charmcraft status lma-light

title: Playing with alertmanager config
content:
Some people get excited by dates such as April 20, 2004, 8:04pm (20/04/2004, 20:04). Well, what if we could have alertmanager fire an alert every time the gregorian calendar looks exciting?
alertmanager does offers quite a powerful configurational flexibility that we could use just for that.
For the purpose of this exercise we will be generating dummy alerts, based on $elapsed=$(date +%s), the number of seconds since 1970-01-01, as follows:
   service alertname label: property     prime $(date +%s) “prime”, if the number is a prime number   palindrome $(date +%s) “palindrome”, if the number is a palindrome   fizzbuzz $(date +%s) “Fizz”, “Buzz” or “FizzBuzz”    Install locally (snap) #  > $ sudo snap install prometheus-alertmanager After which you can see that alertmanager is running with default parameters:
> $ pgrep -a alertmanager /snap/prometheus-alertmanager/11/bin/alertmanager --config.file=/var/snap/prometheus-alertmanager/11/alertmanager.yml --storage.path=/var/snap/prometheus-alertmanager/common Note the default location of the configuration file: cat /var/snap/prometheus-alertmanager/11/alertmanager.yml.
Anatomy of an alert #  The two most important attributes of an alarm are status and labels. In yaml form it looks like this:
status: firing labels: alertname: "name of alert" service: "name of service" severety: "warning" Alerts like this one can be posted to the alertmanager server via HTTP API, obtained via HTTP API or viewed on a web gui via a browser.
Just to get things started, we can easily create a simple alert with the following bash function:
post_alert () { # $1 service # $2 alertname # $3 labels.property curl -XPOST $url -d "[{ \"status\": \"firing\", \"labels\": { \"alertname\": \"$2\", \"service\": \"$1\", \"severity\":\"warning\", \"property\": \"$3\" }, \"annotations\": { \"summary\": \"$1: $2$3\" }, \"generatorURL\": \"http://prometheus.int.example.net\" }]" && echo "" } Alerting model #  For the purpose of this exercise we will be generating dummy alerts, based on the number of seconds since 1970-01-01 (obtained via date +%s), as follows:
   service alertname label: property     prime $(date +%s) “prime”, if the number is a prime number   palindrome $(date +%s) “palindrome”, if the number is a palindrome   fizzbuzz $(date +%s) “Fizz”, “Buzz” or “FizzBuzz”    This way we will have many alerts (possibly duplicated) to play, group and filter with.
alertmanager configuration #  > $ cat /var/snap/prometheus-alertmanager/11/alertmanager.yml route: group_by: ['alertname'] receiver: 'dummy' receivers: - name: 'dummy'    Parameter Purpose     group_by: ['alertname'] what groups divide alerts into   group_wait: 30s collect a bunch of alerts before notifying   group_interval: 5m wait before re-notifying (per group)   repeat_interval: 1h wait before re-notifying (per alert)    The alert-generating script #  while : do elapsed=$(date +%s) is_prime $elapsed is_palindrome $elapsed fizzbuzz $elapsed echo "-------------------" sleep 0.3 done See alerts in a karma dashboard #  The resulting alerts are easy to work with using a karma dashboard. We can notice a few magical things the happened to our alerts:
 Even though we only sleep 0.3 and generate duplicated alerts for the same value of $elapsed, alertmanager de-duplicated those alerts without us even asking for it. The alerts are indeed grouped by alertname: all the alerts for a given value of $elapsed are grouped together. For example, 1626995313 is divisible by 3, its last 3 digits (313) form a palindrome, and its last two digits (13) is a prime number. On the other hand, 1626995330 is divisible by 5 but does not have the other properties. The service that generated the alerts is clearly visible (and can be used for filtering). If we wanted to see all the numbers that are a palindrome, we could group_by: ['service'] and then filter by service=palindrome.  Inhibit rules #  Let’s change the logic of the fizzbuzz function to print ‘Fizz’, ‘Buzz’ AND ‘FizzBuzz’ when the number is divisible by 15. Now we will have three alarms for every ‘FizzBuzz’.
An inhibit rule allows to mute a set of alerts given that another alert is firing. In the FizzBuzz case, we want to mute both Fizz and Buzz if FizzBuzz is already firing:
inhibit_rules: - source_match: service: 'fizzbuzz' property: 'FizzBuzz' target_match_re: service: 'fizzbuzz' property: 'Fizz|Buzz' equal: ['alertname']
